author: @Werevertumoto
text: Mine crashed with the vacuum cleaner reservoir when auto loading the tool in the position 6, some plastics crushed very loudly.

author: @hunterprecis
text: Robert i love your channel.  I don't have this machine but have ran and programmed cnc's for over 30 years and have 2 at home for making parts for high voltage industry.  I would try running the 4th axis as fast as it will run, then run the spindle at its fastest rate.  Then run the tool inward slowly using a 2 flute flat mill slightly off center.  Adjust feed as needed for best finish.  High spiral carbide climb mill should be perfect.  Alcohol mist with air is a good clean coolant.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: what is the designation letter for your 4th axis? (ie normal 3 are XYZ for sideways front to back and up down while 4th would either be A or W as normally its XYZ for motion and rotational being AB or C or UV or W) just curious

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: what is even stranger is that it goes backwards if its U V and W where the 4th 5th and 6th are W V and U while using ABC it goes in normal order of 4th A 5th B 6th C and each one is along the XY or Z (with the rotation of your cutter being really a 6th axis ha ha.)

author: @Tim-Swann
text:
I am sorry to say this comment isn't related to the video but i couldn't think of a better person to ask. I have a mill lathe, 3d printer etc. but nothing i have ever done is strength or weight critical, i tend to over engineer everything and it hasnt been an issue so far. I have go into quadcopters and am looking to make my own frames for tiny drones... under 20g where everything will be critical. Can you tell me what search terms i should be using to find information about the strengths and weaknesses, of different shapes, types of joints, best material practice etc.

I hope you can make some sense of what i am after as without the name its almost impossible to find what i need. Thanks for your time

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It also matters where you search mate - if I am seriously looking into a subject I always turn to google scholar and read some of the latest research - Drone frames are a hot topic right now - that may well be a good place to look

replies.author: @Tim-Swann
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Thank you so much, i hadnt even considered google scholar. I didnt actually look for drone frame design as this is unlike any drone out there, its tiny... its more like a tiny version of a bike frame. Finding things was a lot easier when the internet was smaller, now sorting through results takes longer than learning the piece of info you were after. 

Thank you once again and i hope you like the CNC, it looks like an incredible piece of kit

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @Tim-Swann cheers mate - searching on what they are doing now will key you into the terminology in current use for materials and geometries and should help refine you own searches

author: @gerryplayz4532
text: Great video Rob 😎👍 Can the lathe on it be used as a normal lathe too or is it just for the milling bits? Could you make a 2025 T'n'T coin 🪙 to show how it does with fine detail? It looks like a good machine, I think an automatic bit change would be great, they could do something like 4 numbered bit holders that you load up with the required bits and it could go between them as needed

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a cool idea mate - i may well do that - The air basically looks like a cut down carvera to me and the larger carvera does have a tool change option

replies.author: @gerryplayz4532
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering  ah so Air as in Lite, thanks 😎👍

author: @dav1dbone

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I couldn't find one - i did come acros someone making watch parts but the were the plates not the gears - do you have a title?

replies.author: @dav1dbone
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Hi Rob, it was Myfordboy, "Model Engineering with the Makera Carvera Air Desktop CNC Machine
" if you watch right through to around 13 minutes he starts to set up the 4th axis, he's using the V cutter tool.  There was another video where I saw a bigger gear cut from aluminum, I can't find that now, although I'm inclined to think it might have been one of your videos, so I'm not sure?
